summary: French Academic painter of idealized figures. Known for The Birth of Venus, Dante and Virgil in Hell, Nymphs and Satyr, The Young Shepherdess, The Bohemian, and nude women on seashores. Prominent alongside Alexandre Cabanel. Won Prix de Rome at 25. Wife Elizabeth Jane Gardner imitated his style. John Berger reproduced his Les Oreades in Ways of Seeing.

works.name: The Birth of Venus (1879)
works.description: Title red-haired goddess stands on a conch shell, surrounded by cupids and mermen. Thirteen cherubs appear flying in the background. Two centaurs/mermen blow conch shells. Serpentine central figure lifted from his previous painting The Nymphaeum. Long procession of cupids flying into the sky, as in The Oreads. Wikipedia

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: French Academic painter. Contemporary of Alexandre Cabanel. Won Prix de Rome at 25 with Zenobia Found by Shepherds. Elizabeth Jane Gardner married him and imitated his style. Painted innocent-looking girls including The Young Shepherdess (with violin), The Bohemian (with violin, Notre-Dame in background), Alone in the World, The Knitting Girl. Associated with Academic art and l'art pompier. John Berger reproduced Les Oreades in Ways of Seeing.

clues.clue: Leading practitioner of Academic art, alongside Alexandre Cabanel
examples: Bouguereau and Cabanel were prominent members of this largely French artistic movement
Bouguereau was a leading practitioner of this style of art, which favored idealized, allegorical subjects

clues.clue: Painted portraits of innocent young girls: The Young Shepherdess, The Bohemian, Alone in the World, The Knitting Girl

used the same young model in his paintings The Shepherdess and The Bohemian

clues.clue: Academic art was derisively called 'l'art pompier' (fireman art) due to Attic helmets resembling firemen's headgear
examples: Critics savaged academism by labeling it as this kind of 'art.' The use of this French term derived from the fact that certain public safety officials in France wore headgear similar to the Attic helmets shown in academic art

comprehensive_summary:
William-Adolphe Bouguereau (1825-1905) was a French Academic painter of the 19th century, one of the leading practitioners of Academic art alongside Alexandre Cabanel (frequency 5, giveaway). He painted idealized depictions of the human figure and was famous for genre scenes of innocent young girls (frequency 4).

His most iconic painting, The Birth of Venus (1879), depicts the title red-haired goddess standing on a conch shell surrounded by cupids, mermen, and nymphs. Thirteen cherubs fly in the background, and two centaurs blow conch shells. The serpentine central figure was lifted from his previous painting The Nymphaeum. A long procession of cupids flies into the sky, similar to the composition of The Oreads.

Dante and Virgil in Hell (frequency 4 from text mentions) shows a naked man kneeing another in the back and biting his throat/neck while the two poets watch. This painting was submitted for Bouguereau's third attempt to win the Prix de Rome. His Nymphs and Satyr shows four naked women dragging a faun into a pool.

Bouguereau painted many portraits of innocent young girls: The Young Shepherdess (girl looking over her shoulder, sheep in background, leafy twig in hand), The Bohemian (shawled barefoot girl on a concrete bench with a violin on her lap, Notre-Dame de Paris visible in background), Alone in the World, and The Knitting Girl. He used the same young brunette model for several of these.

He won the Prix de Rome at age 25 with Zenobia Found by Shepherds on the Banks of the Araxes. His wife Elizabeth Jane Gardner imitated his style, stating she'd 'rather be known as the best imitator of [him] than be nobody.' His painting First Mourning (Adam and Eve discovering Abel's body) was painted after the death of his own son.

Bouguereau and Cabanel refused to allow the Impressionists into the Paris Salon of 1863, leading to the Salon des Refuses. Academic art was derisively called 'l'art pompier' (fireman art) because Attic helmets resembled firemen's headgear. John Berger's Ways of Seeing reproduced his Les Oreades to illustrate how men of state discussed under such paintings. Other notable works include Springtime (three cherubs tend to a woman in a forest), The Wave (1896), a fury painting (topless figure holding torch, pointing at stabbed woman), The Flagellation of Christ (figure bound to a column), and the Abduction of Psyche (wrapped in purple cloth).
